context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Global Poverty

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How has development come to be associated popularly with poverty alleviation? What do we make of techniques of poverty alleviation such as microfinance, in terms of the theories of economic development? Is it possible to focus on poverty successfully in a global economy characterized by hyper-capitalism? Are the techniques for measuring poverty, and the goal-setting exercised like MDG, satisfactory?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection for my international development planning class about global poverty. I’m kind of stuck on how to connect poverty alleviation, microfinance, and bigger development theories without making it sound all over the place.
exchanges.tutor:
Good starting point — let's dig in. Before thinking about structure, let's make sure the ideas are clear.

Here's a simple question to get us going: In your own words, why do you think 'development' and 'poverty alleviation' are so often talked about as if they mean the same thing? Do you think they actually do?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting out and expressing a general organizational concern. Rather than giving them a structure or outline (which would be doing the work for them), I should help them engage with the actual content first. The best Socratic move is to ask them what they already know or think about one of the core questions in the prompt, to get them thinking and talking. This will reveal their current understanding and help me guide them from there.

exchanges.student: I think they get treated like the same thing because poverty is the most visible and morally urgent problem, so “development” gets reduced to helping poor people. But I don’t think they fully mean the same thing, since development is bigger—it could include structural change, institutions, power, and long-term economic transformation, not just raising incomes.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid distinction. Now push it further: if development is really about structural change and institutions, where does something like microfinance fit in? Does it address those deeper dimensions, or does it operate at a different level entirely?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given a strong and perceptive answer that distinguishes between poverty alleviation and broader development. I should affirm this without over-praising, and push them to the next level by connecting this distinction to actual development theories covered in the course. This moves the conversation toward the second part of the prompt about microfinance and theories of economic development.

exchanges.student: I think microfinance works at a much smaller level—it helps individuals or households get access to credit, but that’s not the same as changing the structure of the economy. So maybe it fits more with a neoliberal idea that poor people can “self-improve” through markets, instead of the state addressing inequality or lack of jobs. I’m not totally sure if that’s too harsh, though.
exchanges.tutor: That's not too harsh — it's a real debate in the field. But test your own argument: if microfinance helps someone start a small business, what structural conditions still remain unchanged around them? What does that tell you about the limits of market-based solutions to poverty?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a genuinely good theoretical connection between microfinance and neoliberal ideology. They're also showing appropriate uncertainty, which is intellectually honest. I should validate the direction without endorsing it as complete, and push them to think about whether their critique holds up — specifically by asking them to consider what microfinance does and doesn't change at the structural level. This moves toward the third prompt question about hyper-capitalism.

exchanges.student: I guess things like land ownership, labor conditions, infrastructure, access to education, and who actually controls markets would still stay the same. So even if one person gets a loan, they’re still operating inside a system that might keep them poor or vulnerable. I think that means market-based solutions can help at the margins but maybe can’t fix the causes of poverty by themselves.
exchanges.tutor:
Good — you're essentially arguing that poverty is produced by structures, not just individual lack of resources. That has real implications for your reflection.

So here's the next question: if the global economy under hyper-capitalism actually generates and reproduces those structural conditions you just described, what does that mean for efforts to 'focus on poverty' within that same system? Can you fix the symptom without addressing the cause?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is building a coherent argument about structural constraints versus individual-level interventions. This is excellent progress. Now I should connect this to the third prompt question about hyper-capitalism — which they're already implicitly touching on — and then nudge them toward the fourth question about measurement tools like the MDGs. I want to keep moving through the prompt without dwelling too long on this point.

exchanges.student: I’m leaning toward saying not really, or at least not fully. It feels like anti-poverty programs can reduce some immediate suffering, but if hyper-capitalism depends on inequality, cheap labor, and uneven development, then poverty keeps getting reproduced. I’m just not sure how absolute to sound—I don’t want to claim nothing helps.
exchanges.tutor:
That tension you're feeling is actually the honest position — and it's worth holding onto in your writing. You don't have to resolve it, just be clear about what you're arguing and why.

Let's move to the last part of the prompt: what do you think about tools like the MDGs and poverty measurement techniques? Do they capture what actually matters, or do they have blind spots?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing good nuance and intellectual honesty. They're navigating a real tension in development theory between structural critique and pragmatic intervention. I should affirm the nuance without resolving it for them, and push them to think about how to express that tension in their writing — without writing it for them. Then I should move them toward the last prompt question about MDGs and measurement, since we're about halfway through our planned exchanges.

exchanges.student: I think they’re useful because they make poverty legible and give governments/institutions targets, but they also seem narrow. If poverty gets measured mostly by income or simple indicators, then power, insecurity, informal labor, exclusion, and local context can get missed. So I think they help with visibility, but they can also simplify development into technocratic benchmarks.
exchanges.tutor:
Good — that 'legibility vs. simplification' tension is a strong critical point.

You've now worked through all four parts of the prompt. Before you start writing, can you tell me in one or two sentences: what's the central argument that ties all these ideas together? What's the one thing you want your reader to take away?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now addressed all four parts of the prompt with solid thinking. They've touched on: the conflation of development and poverty alleviation, microfinance and neoliberal theory, structural limits under hyper-capitalism, and MDG/measurement critiques. We've had 5 exchanges and the student has good raw material. It's time to push them toward integration — asking them to start thinking about how these ideas connect into a coherent reflection piece, without me doing the synthesizing for them.

exchanges.student: I think my main argument is that development became popularly equated with poverty alleviation because poverty is easier to see and measure, but that narrows development into a technical problem instead of a structural and political one. Things like microfinance and the MDGs can help in limited ways, but in a hyper-capitalist global economy they often manage poverty more than transform the conditions that produce it.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong, coherent thesis. Now the work is to build the reflection around it.

One thing to keep in mind as you write: you'll need to ground your claims in specific ideas or examples, not just general statements. What concrete examples or thinkers from your course readings could you draw on to support your argument?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a clear and well-integrated central argument that genuinely ties together all four parts of the prompt. This is excellent progress. I should affirm it briefly and push them to start writing, since we're at exchange 7 and the goal is for them to produce the reflection piece, not just talk about it.

exchanges.student: I’m blanking a little on specific names right now. I vaguely remember critiques of microfinance and also ideas about development being tied to modernization or later to basic needs/poverty reduction, but I need help remembering what kinds of readings or theorists would fit each part.
exchanges.tutor:
That's something you'll need to go back to your syllabus and readings for — I won't supply the names for you, since engaging with the actual texts is part of the assignment.

But as a starting point: think about what your class has covered on modernization theory, the shift to basic needs approaches, and critiques of market-based development. Do any readings or authors come to mind when you think about those topics?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to supply names and readings for them, which would essentially be doing part of their research and writing work. That's not my role. I should redirect them to their course materials and use a Socratic nudge to help them retrieve what they already know, rather than providing a list of theorists or readings for them.

exchanges.student: Maybe modernization theory would connect to earlier ideas of development as growth and transformation, and then the basic needs shift would explain why poverty became more central later. For critiques of market-based development, I think I should look for whoever we read on neoliberalism, participation, or maybe aid/governance, because that probably overlaps with the microfinance critique.
exchanges.tutor:
That's exactly the right instinct — go back to those readings and match them to the parts of your argument. You have a clear thesis and a solid sense of the terrain.

At this point, the most useful thing you can do is start drafting. Once you have a first attempt, I'm happy to help you think through whether your argument is coming through clearly.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track in terms of knowing where to look, even if they can't recall specific names yet. This is good self-directed thinking. I should affirm their approach briefly and push them to actually go do that reading review and start drafting, since we're at exchange 8 and the conversation has been productive enough. The student has the argument, the structure, and the search strategy — now they need to write.

exchanges.student: Okay, that helps. I’m going to draft around that thesis and then come back if my paragraphs start feeling disconnected.
exchanges.tutor: Good plan. One thing to watch: make sure each paragraph is doing work for your central argument, not just describing what microfinance or MDGs are. Come back when you have a draft!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is wrapping up and ready to draft, which is the right move at this stage. I should give a brief, encouraging send-off that reinforces one key thing to watch for without over-instructing. Keep it short.
